headline: Spanish Donkeys Keep Wildfires at Bay, One Mouthful at a Time
meta_description: Meet the gentle four-legged team protecting Spain's forests. Donkeys are proving far more effective than traditional methods at preventing devastating wildfire…
summary_short: In Spain's Doñana National Park, a team of hardworking donkeys has kept wildfires at bay for 9 years simply by grazing. Now other regions are following suit.
body:
While much of Europe sweltered under a recent heat dome, Spain's Doñana National Park remained lush and safe—thanks to an unlikely workforce grazing across its fire breaks.

Donkeys named Leonor, Ainoa, and Ume spend seven hours daily chomping through the shrubs and grasses that would otherwise fuel catastrophic wildfires. The park hasn't experienced a wildfire in nine years, a record increasingly attributed to these patient animals.

The concept is elegantly simple: as the donkeys eat, they clear away the tinder-dry vegetation that accumulates in Spain's naturally parched pine forests. What makes donkeys superior to sheep and goats? Weight and appetite. A donkey weighs three times more than a goat and eats roughly ten times as much, meaning its daily impact on the landscape is dramatically greater.

"Since we introduced donkeys in our municipality, there have been no wildfires," says Joan Cedó, who launched Catalonia's Tivissa Donkeys Firefighters in 2020.

The success has inspired action across Spain. Galicia, Catalonia, Ourense, Navarre, and the Basque Country have all enlisted their own donkey brigades. What researchers increasingly recognize is that the absence of such grazing animals—once commonplace across the Iberian landscape—has left forests dangerously overgrown.

Sometimes the most effective solution is also the most ancient.
